taskVersion.name: epu_q2_long
taskVersion.definition:
@kbench.task(name="epu_q2_long")
def epu_q2_long(
    llm,
    article_key: str,
    content_for_eval: str,
    gold_epu: int,
    short_pre: str = "",
    short_post: str = "",
    medium_pre: str = "",
    medium_post: str = "",
    long_pre: str = "",
    long_post: str = "",
    disagreement_flag: int = 0,
) -> float:
    out = model_json_call(llm, build_q2_prompt(content_for_eval, long_pre, long_post))
    m = binary_match(out["epu"], gold_epu)
    return 0.0 if m is None else float(m)

parts.text:
You are evaluating only the text between TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END.

Task:
Decide whether the TARGET ARTICLE expresses ECONOMIC POLICY UNCERTAINTY (EPU).

Labeling rule:
- EPU = 1 only if the TARGET ARTICLE discusses uncertainty about government policy,
  policy decisions, possible policy actions, political outcomes affecting policy,
  or the economic effects of policy.
- EPU = 0 for general economics, elections, markets, war, uncertainty, or foreign events
  unless the uncertainty is specifically tied to economic policy.

IMPORTANT:
- Use only the text inside TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END for the decision.
- Return ONLY valid JSON.
- No markdown. No explanation. No code fence.

Return exactly these keys:
{
  "epu": 0 or 1,
  "confidence": number between 0 and 1,
  "focus_excerpt": "short quote from target article only"
}

TARGET ARTICLE START
Estimates Reduced On 2d-Half Growth Estimates Reduced On 2d-Half Growth By NICHOLAS D. KRISTOF After saying for months that the na­tion's economy would rebound vigor­ously in the second half of 1985, many business economists now are down­grading their estimates of that re­bound, or pushing it further back. Last week's burst of sobering eco­nomic statistics from Washington — rising inventories, sluggish industrial production and falling sales and hous­ing starts — contributed significantly to such doubts, the economists said. "We're all rethinking our posi­tions," said Michael W. Keran, chief economist at the Prudential Insur­ance Company of America. "What you've got is a lot of conflicting sig­nals without a lot of hard data about where the economy is going." Mr. Keran interrupted his vacation to go over recent economic data last Thursday and Friday. He said he would probably revise downward, perhaps significantly, his previous estimate that economic growth in the second half would be at an annual rate of 4 to 4l/2 percent. Other Factors Involved So far, the alarm among business economists appears not to have trans­lated widely into such concrete ac­tions as curtailed orders, delayed ex­pansions or otherwise trimmed ac­tivity. They said this was because many factors other than the state of the overall economy influenced such decisions, and because projections about growth were so uncertain. "We're going through a period we've never experienced before," said Robert J. Corkhill

[...TRUNCATED...]

aid Rosalind Wells, chief economist of the J.C. Penney Company. "So we wouldn't pull back." "I don't think inventories are see as unduly high in industry," said John Deaver, chief economist of the Ford Motor Company. Other econo­mists also noted that the btost in in­ventories in June came at the whole­sale and manufacturing level, while retail inventories fell. That could mean that any problem was cor­rected as retailers continued in July and August to order more gtods. Mr. Deaver added that the concern about the economy's prospects had probably spread to the Federal Re­serve System. The Federal Open Market Committee, which controls sales and parehases of securities by the Fed as a way of stimulating or reining in the economy, will meet tomorrow in Washington and could decide to ease monetary policy, he said. "My guess is that they are pretty nervous at the Fed right now," Mr. Deaver added. "There may be some on the board arguing for easier, more accommodative policies." The dilemma facing the Fed was exacerbated by last Thursday's an­nouncement that the narrowest measure of the nation's money supply had soared $5.3 billion in the week ended Aug. 5. Some economists said that the unusually large increase, on top of a generally rapid btost in the basic money supply so far this year, might make the Fed wary of a further easing of monetary policy. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.
TARGET ARTICLE END

{"epu": 1, "confidence": 0.72, "focus_excerpt": "The Federal Open Market Committee, which controls sales and purchases of securities by the Fed as a way of stimulating or reining in the economy, will meet tomorrow in Washington and could decide to ease monetary policy...
